one_line_takeaway: ACTHOOK embeds behavior-level 'hook action' watermarks at high-entropy action boundaries in LLM agent trajectory datasets, achieving high black-box detection AUC (Standalone avg 97.8, Contextual avg 90.8 on Qwen-2.5-Coder-7B) with negligible task-performance cost, though Contextual watermarks lose most of their signal under paraphrasing on the more complex SWE-Smith setting (AUC 94.2 to 56.0).
key_finding: On Qwen-2.5-Coder-7B with N=1 prompt and Q=8 queries, ACTHOOK's Standalone and Contextual variants average 97.8 and 90.8 AUC respectively across the three datasets, versus CodeMark's 55.5 (near chance). Per-dataset breakdown (Standalone / Contextual / CodeMark): MATH 99.5 / 96.3 / 56.7; SimpleQA 99.6 / 87.9 / 56.6; SWE-Smith 88.3 / 94.2 / 53.1. The paper's abstract-level headline of 'average detection AUC of 94.3' corresponds to the mean of the Standalone (97.8) and Contextual (90.8) averages. Performance preservation: across model scales (3B/7B/14B) and datasets, ACTHOOK causes only small Pass@1 changes relative to the unwatermarked fine-tuned model ('w/o wmk'), e.g. on Qwen-2.5-Coder-7B MATH: 75.3% (w/o wmk) vs 75.3%/75.3% (Standalone/Contextual); SimpleQA: 75.8% vs 75.3%/77.1%; SWE-Bench: 13.0% vs 12.3%/12.7%. Robustness results are mixed rather than uniformly strong: under DeCoMa filtering, ACTHOOK keeps AUC in the 88-100 range at R=0.05/0.10 while CodeMark collapses to near-50 AUC with much higher (but still weak, ~30-45%) precision/recall. Under the paraphrase attack, Standalone AUC is essentially preserved or improved (e.g. MATH 99.5->99.8, SimpleQA 99.6->99.7, SWE 88.3->94.5), but Contextual degrades meaningfully -- most sharply on SWE-Smith, where AUC falls from 94.2 to 56.0 (a 38.2-point drop, delta-q falling from 0.71 to 0.28) -- contradicting a literal reading of the abstract's claim that 'AUC remains above 85 after ... paraphrasing.' Action summarization has only a small effect (average delta-q change of -0.038, AUC drop of about 1 point). Continued fine-tuning on clean data degrades detection only marginally while |D_c|/|D_o| < 60%, with degradation compensable by increasing query count N.
core_contribution: The paper introduces ACTHOOK, the first watermarking method designed specifically for LLM agent trajectory datasets (as opposed to plain text or standalone code). Instead of token-level or syntactic watermarks, ACTHOOK inserts auxiliary 'hook actions' at action boundaries in a trajectory (inspired by software hooks such as Python's sys.settrace() or Git pre-commit hooks), paired with a secret activation key appended to the user prompt. An agent fine-tuned (behavior-cloned) on the watermarked trajectories learns to emit these hook actions at a significantly higher rate when the activation key is present in a query, which enables black-box provenance detection by comparing hook-action emergence rates with vs. without the key, formalized as a statistical hypothesis test (paired one-sided t-test against a 'sham key' null, plus a sample-complexity theorem bounding queries needed for target FPR/FNR). The method has two hook variants: standalone (context-independent, e.g. a pwd/version-check command) and contextual (conditioned on trajectory content, e.g. an 'ls -la' after a file-creation step). Injection is post-hoc on collected trajectories via a filter-sample-inject pipeline (W.CHECK / W.INJECT / W.DETECT), using an auxiliary LLM (Qwen-3-Coder-30B-A3B) to generate diverse hook-action phrasings.
threat_model: Defender: a dataset creator who has produced a costly, high-quality LLM-agent trajectory dataset (math reasoning, web search, or SWE-bench-style code-fixing trajectories) and wants to detect unauthorized downstream use (e.g., commercial deployment or license-violating redistribution) after releasing the dataset. Detection is black-box: the defender only queries the suspect agent with crafted prompts (with/without the activation key) and observes output actions -- no access to weights or training data of the suspect model. Adversary: a party who obtains the released (watermarked) trajectory dataset and trains/fine-tunes an agent on it, potentially attempting to strip the watermark before or after training via (a) DeCoMa-style filtering that maps actions to templates and flags anomalous ones, (b) LLM-based paraphrasing of trajectories explicitly instructed to remove any watermark, (c) action summarization (only a semantic summary of each action is shown/used, discarding exact wording), or (d) continued fine-tuning on additional clean data to dilute the learned watermark behavior. The paper also references (in appendices, not read in full here) attacks that target the hook actions or the activation key directly.
limitations: The paper's own reported numbers already show Contextual watermarks are not robust to paraphrasing on SWE-Smith (AUC 94.2 -> 56.0, close to chance), so the claimed general robustness to watermark-removal attacks is watermark-type- and dataset-dependent, not universal -- this nuance is present in Table 4 but is glossed over in the abstract's single 'AUC remaining above 85' figure. SWE-Smith detection is consistently the weakest setting for Standalone watermarks (AUC 88.3 on 7B, only 52-59 at 3B model scale per the paper's hyperparameter analysis), attributed by the authors to task complexity leaving less model capacity to learn the watermark. Detection requires the defender to already suspect a specific model/agent and issue black-box queries with the correct activation key; the paper does not claim to detect watermark presence without knowing the key. Watermark strength depends on model scale (3B agents show only modest AUC separation, especially on SWE-Smith) and on watermark ratio R (R=0.05 is the default; lower ratios such as R=0.01-0.02 show much weaker signal per Figure 5, especially for Contextual variants). The authors' own Impact Statement flags residual risks: possible false accusations from statistical detection under uncertainty, and the possibility that publishing the method aids adversaries in designing better removal attacks.

datasets_used: MATH (1,000 sampled trajectories, Smolagents scaffolding, DeepSeek-V3/Qwen-3-235B-A22B generated, filtered to correct answers, evaluated on Smolagents-Benchmark-v1)
SimpleQA (2,000 sampled trajectories, Smolagents scaffolding, same generators, evaluated on Smolagents-Benchmark-v1)
SWE-Smith (2,000 sampled trajectories derived from real GitHub issues, generated by Claude-3.7-Sonnet, SWE-Agent scaffolding, evaluated on SWE-Bench Lite)
quantitative_results: Average AUC on Qwen-2.5-Coder-7B (N=1, Q=8): Standalone 97.8, Contextual 90.8, CodeMark 55.5
Per-dataset AUC on Qwen-2.5-Coder-7B (Standalone/Contextual/CodeMark): MATH 99.5/96.3/56.7; SimpleQA 99.6/87.9/56.6; SWE-Smith 88.3/94.2/53.1
Pass@1 preservation on Qwen-2.5-Coder-7B: MATH 75.3% (w/o wmk) vs 75.3%/75.3% (Standalone/Contextual); SimpleQA 75.8% vs 75.3%/77.1%; SWE-Bench 13.0% vs 12.3%/12.7%
DeCoMa filtering at R=0.05: ACTHOOK AUC 97.3 (MATH Standalone)/95.8 (MATH Contextual)/98.8 (SimpleQA Standalone)/88.3 (SimpleQA Contextual) vs CodeMark 53.9 (MATH)/50.0 (SimpleQA)
Paraphrase attack AUC change: Standalone MATH 99.5->99.8, SimpleQA 99.6->99.7, SWE 88.3->94.5; Contextual MATH 96.3->91.5, SimpleQA 87.9->83.1, SWE 94.2->56.0 (largest drop, -38.2)
Action summarization attack: average delta-q change -0.038, average AUC drop ~1 percentage point across datasets and watermark types
Continued fine-tuning on clean data (SimpleQA): AUC degrades only marginally while |D_c|/|D_o| < 60%
Sample-complexity theorem: required query count n scales as O(1/delta_q^2) for target false-positive rate alpha and false-negative rate beta
baselines_compared: CodeMark (Sun et al. 2023) -- primary baseline, semantic-preserving code transformation watermark adapted by appending flush=True (Python) or 2>&1 (Bash)
AutoPoison (Shu et al. 2023) -- backdoor-based baseline, evaluated on MATH and SimpleQA
DeadCode (Li et al. 2023b) -- backdoor-based baseline, evaluated on MATH and SimpleQA
StyleTransfer (Pan et al. 2022) -- backdoor-based baseline, evaluated on MATH and SimpleQA
DeCoMa (Xiao et al. 2025) -- used as a watermark-removal/filtering attack against ACTHOOK and CodeMark, not a detection baseline
